Suggest a new scanning probe measurement.answer: One possibility is “Scanning Photoacoustic Resonance Microscopy (SPRM),” where a tip-induced photoacoustic signal is detected locally to probe both mechanical and optical properties with high spatial resolution.

“Scanning Thermoplasmonic Microscopy (STPM),” which uses a heated plasmonic tip to locally excite and detect thermoplasmonic interactions, enabling simultaneous measurement of optical and thermal properties at high spatial resolution.
Scanning Polariton Force Microscopy (SPFM), which uses a specialized tip to locally excite and detect polariton modes for high-resolution mapping of electromagnetic and vibrational properties.

“Scanning Magnetoelastic Resonance Microscopy (SMRM),” which employs a magnetized tip to locally excite and detect magnetoelastic resonances, enabling high-resolution mapping of coupled magnetic and mechanical properties.

“Scanning Quantum Dot Coupling Microscopy (SQDCM),” where a tip-integrated quantum dot sensor locally probes exciton-coupling and electron transfer at the sample surface for high-resolution optical and electronic characterization.

“Scanning Ion-Photon Coupling Microscopy (SIPCM),” which employs a tip integrating an ion-conducting channel with an embedded optical waveguide to probe local ion transport and photon-matter interactions simultaneously at the nanoscale.

“Scanning Ultrafast Carrier Dynamics Microscopy (SUCDM),” which uses a femtosecond-laser-pulsed tip to locally excite and detect electron–hole processes, enabling high-resolution mapping of ultrafast phenomena in materials.

“Scanning Spin-Orbit Coupling Force Microscopy (SSOCFM),” which uses a specialized spin-polarized tip to locally probe and modulate spin-orbit interactions, enabling nanoscale mapping of spin textures and electron momentum locking in advanced materials.

“Scanning Ion-Lattice Coupling Microscopy (SILCM),” which employs a tip capable of localized ion injection and displacement detection to reveal how ionic perturbations affect crystal lattice structures at the nanoscale.

“Scanning Josephson Coupling Microscopy (SJCM),” which employs a tip-based micro-SQUID to locally probe superconducting phase coherence and critical currents, revealing nanoscale superconducting properties in complex materials.

“Scanning Nonlinear Optical Force Microscopy (SNOFM),” which employs a tip capable of locally exciting and sensing nonlinear optical processes (e.g., second-harmonic generation), enabling nanoscale mapping of materials’ nonlinear responses with high spatial resolution.

“Scanning Exciton–Phonon Coupling Microscopy (SEPCM),” which employs a specialized tip to locally excite and detect exciton–phonon interactions, revealing nanoscale coupling phenomena in semiconductors and other advanced materials.

“Scanning Mie-Resonance Force Microscopy (SMRFM),” which employs a specialized tip to excite and detect local Mie resonances in the sample, enabling nanoscale mapping of scattering dynamics and near-field electromagnetic responses.

“Scanning Rydberg Excitation Microscopy (SREM),” which employs a tip-based Rydberg atom probe to locally excite high principal quantum number states and measure subtle variations in the electromagnetic environment at the nanoscale.

“Scanning Brillouin Force Microscopy (SBFM),” which employs a specialized tip to locally stimulate and detect Brillouin scattering, enabling high-resolution mapping of mechanical and elastic properties through acoustic mode interactions at the nanoscale.

“Scanning Chiral Resonance Force Microscopy (SCRFM),” employing a tip capable of locally exciting chiroptical resonances in the sample, enabling nanoscale mapping of molecular or structural chirality.

“Scanning Thermoelectric Coupling Microscopy (STECM),” which employs a tip generating a localized temperature gradient and measuring the resulting local thermoelectric voltage, so as to map nanoscale variations in thermoelectric properties.

“Scanning Nuclear Quadrupole Resonance Microscopy (SNQRM),” which employs a tip-based resonator to locally detect nuclear quadrupole signals, enabling nanoscale mapping of subtle electronic environments and atomic-scale structural distortions.

“Scanning Parametric Down-Conversion Microscopy (SPDCM),” which uses a tip that locally facilitates nonlinear photon-pair generation and detection, enabling nanoscale mapping of quantum-level optical interactions.

“Scanning Topological Edge State Microscopy (STESM),” which employs a specialized tip to locally probe and induce boundary-state conduction in topological materials, enabling nanoscale mapping of protected surface or edge channels.
“Scanning Electrocaloric Force Microscopy (SECFM),” which uses a specialized tip to induce and detect local electrocaloric effects, enabling nanoscale mapping of ferroelectric and thermal couplings.

“Scanning Plasma Frequency Force Microscopy (SPFFM),” which uses a specialized tip to locally excite and detect plasma resonances, enabling high-resolution mapping of conduction electron dynamics and permittivity variations.

“Scanning Magnon–Photon Coupling Microscopy (SMPCM),” which uses a specialized tip to locally excite and detect coupled spin-wave and electromagnetic modes, enabling nanoscale mapping of spin–photonic interactions.

“Scanning Valley-Polarization Microscopy (SVPM),” which employs a specialized tip to locally excite and probe valley-selective transitions, enabling nanoscale mapping of valley dependencies in 2D materials.

“Scanning Spin-Caloritronic Force Microscopy (SSCFM),” which uses a specialized tip to locally measure spin-polarized currents driven by thermal gradients, thereby mapping the interplay between spin transport and temperature variations at the nanoscale.

“Scanning Mössbauer Force Microscopy (SMFM),” which uses a tip-integrated gamma-ray resonant absorber to locally measure hyperfine interactions for nanoscale mapping of magnetic and electronic environments.

“Scanning Electron-Hydrodynamic Microscopy (SEHM),” which utilizes a tip to locally excite and sense electron fluid flow, enabling nanoscale mapping of collective charge transport and viscosity in materials exhibiting hydrodynamic electron behavior.

“Scanning Dynamic Coulomb Correlation Microscopy (SDCCM),” which employs a tip-based, time-resolved detection of local electron–electron interactions to reveal correlated electronic behaviors at the nanoscale.

“Scanning Polaronic Distortion Force Microscopy (SPDFM),” which uses a specialized tip to locally probe and map polaron-induced lattice distortions, revealing electron–lattice coupling effects at the nanoscale.

“Scanning Squeezed-Photon Force Microscopy (SSPFM),” which uses a tip-engineered squeezed-light source to locally probe quantum-limit electromagnetic field variations, enabling nanoscale mapping of subtle photonic interactions beyond the shot-noise limit.

“Scanning Photon Drag Microscopy (SPDM),” which uses a specialized tip to locally measure photon-driven momentum transfer in the sample, enabling nanoscale mapping of photon drag effects and associated charge transport phenomena.

“Scanning Acoustic Plasmon Microscopy (SAPM),” which employs a specialized tip to locally excite and detect low-frequency plasmon modes (acoustic plasmons) in layered or low-dimensional conductors, enabling nanoscale mapping of their dispersion and confinement.

“Scanning Non-Hermitian Photonic Microscopy (SNHPM),” which employs a specialized tip to locally modulate and detect gain–loss responses in photonic structures, enabling nanoscale exploration of exceptional points and other non-Hermitian wave phenomena.

“Scanning Kondo-Lattice Force Microscopy (SKLFM),” which employs a specialized tip to locally probe the Kondo interaction between conduction electrons and localized spins in heavy-fermion systems, enabling nanoscale mapping of correlated electronic states.

“Scanning Hyper-Raman Force Microscopy (SHRFM),” which uses a specialized tip to locally excite and detect third-order hyper-Raman scattering for nanoscale mapping of higher-order vibrational and electronic properties.

“Scanning Synthetic-Dimension Photonic Microscopy (SSDPM),” which uses a specialized tip to locally excite and probe synthetic dimension-based photonic modes, enabling nanoscale mapping of higher-dimensional light–matter interactions in advanced photonic circuits.

“Scanning Electron Transfer Reaction Microscopy (SETRM),” which uses a specialized electrochemical tip to locally initiate and detect electron-transfer events, enabling nanoscale mapping of reaction kinetics and redox behavior in materials and interfaces.

“Scanning Fano Resonance Microscopy (SFRM),” which uses a specialized tip to locally induce interference between discrete and continuum states, allowing high-resolution mapping of Fano-type resonances and their sensitivity to the sample’s electronic or photonic environment.

“Scanning Purcell Enhancement Microscopy (SPEM),” which employs a tip-based nanoscale optical cavity to locally modify and measure spontaneous emission rates, mapping the photonic density of states and exciton lifetimes at high spatial resolution.

“Scanning Piezophotonic Microscopy (SPPM),” which applies localized mechanical stress via a specialized tip while measuring shifts in photonic signals, enabling nanoscale mapping of stress-driven optical responses.

“Scanning Photon Autocorrelation Microscopy (SPAM),” which integrates a tip-based single-photon correlation detector to measure local second-order coherence, enabling nanoscale mapping of quantum optical interactions.

“Scanning Nonreciprocal Light–Matter Microscopy (SNLMM),” which employs a specialized tip to locally excite and detect magneto-chiral or magneto-optical effects, enabling nanoscale mapping of broken symmetries.

“Scanning Synthetic Gauge Field Microscopy (SSGFM),” which uses a specialized tip to locally induce artificial gauge fields in quantum materials, enabling nanoscale mapping of emergent topological behaviors.

“Scanning Phonon Hall Microscopy (SPHM),” which employs a specialized tip to locally excite and detect phonon Hall signals in magnetic or topological insulators, enabling nanoscale mapping of chiral phonon transport and thermal conduction anomalies.

“Scanning Floquet Engineering Microscopy (SFEM),” which employs a tip-based time-periodic drive to locally modulate electronic bands, enabling nanoscale mapping of transient, driven-state properties and Floquet-induced phase transitions.

“Scanning Molecular Orbital Force Microscopy (SMOFM),” which uses a specialized tip to locally excite and measure shifts in molecular orbital energies, enabling high-resolution mapping of electronic states in complex molecules.

“Scanning Spin-Echo Force Microscopy (SSEFM),” which employs a tip to locally apply and detect spin-echo sequences, enabling nanoscale mapping of spin coherence times and dynamics in quantum materials.

“Scanning Thermionic Emission Force Microscopy (STEFM),” employing a heated tip to probe local thermionic emission currents, enabling nanoscale mapping of electron work functions and emission dynamics.

“Scanning Axion Magnetoelectric Microscopy (SAMM),” which employs a specialized tip to locally detect and quantify axion-like magnetoelectric couplings in topological materials, enabling nanoscale mapping of emergent electromagnetic phenomena.

“Scanning Superradiant Coupling Microscopy (SSCM),” which uses a tip-integrated resonant nanocavity to locally induce strong light–matter coupling and detect collective emission phenomena, enabling nanoscale mapping of cooperative quantum optical effects.

“Scanning Photo-Induced Spin Injection Microscopy (SPiSIM),” which employs a tip-based spin-polarized light source to locally excite and inject spins into the sample, enabling nanoscale mapping of spin transport and recombination dynamics.

“Scanning Orbital Angular Momentum Microscopy (SOAM),” which employs a specialized tip capable of locally generating or detecting beams with orbital angular momentum, enabling nanoscale mapping of phase singularities and torque interactions in advanced photonic or spin systems.

“Scanning Berry Curvature Force Microscopy (SBCFM),” which employs a specialized tip to locally sense Berry curvature–induced forces, enabling nanoscale mapping of topological and spin–orbit coupling effects in quantum materials.

“Scanning Photocatalytic Reaction Microscopy (SPCRM),” employing a tip-delivered light source and reaction detection to map local photocatalytic activity at the nanoscale, revealing spatial variations in reaction rates and efficiencies.

“Scanning Ionization Potential Force Microscopy (SIPFM),” which uses a tip to locally measure the field threshold required for electron emission, enabling nanoscale mapping of surfaces’ ionization potentials.

“Scanning Spin-Liquid Proximity Microscopy (SSLPM),” which uses a specialized tip to locally sense fractional spin excitations and emergent gauge fields in quantum spin liquids, providing nanoscale mapping of these exotic states.

“Scanning Landau-Level Force Microscopy (SLLFM),” which uses a specialized tip to locally sense and map Landau quantization in samples under a strong magnetic field, revealing nanoscale electronic structure indicative of quantum Hall states.

“Scanning Fracton Transport Microscopy (SFTM),” which uses a specialized tip to locally excite and track fracton-like quasiparticle motion in strongly interacting or fractal systems, enabling nanoscale mapping of subdimensional transport and correlated excitations.

“Scanning Plasmon–Phonon Coupling Microscopy (SPPCM),” which employs a specialized tip to locally excite and detect charge-density oscillations (plasmons) and lattice vibrations (phonons), enabling high-resolution mapping of their coupling and emergent properties in materials.
“Scanning X-Ray Photoemission Force Microscopy (SXPFM),” which employs a tip-level detection of X-ray-induced photoelectrons to enable nanoscale mapping of electronic states and surface chemistry.

“Scanning Soliton Force Microscopy (SSFM),” which uses a specialized tip to locally generate and detect soliton excitations, thereby mapping soliton dynamics and domain-wall interactions at the nanoscale.

“Scanning Spin-Lattice Relaxation Microscopy (SSLRM),” which uses a specialized tip to locally measure T₁ spin-lattice relaxation times, providing nanoscale insight into magnetic relaxation pathways and defect-driven spin dynamics.

“Scanning Metal–Insulator Transition Force Microscopy (SMITFM),” which employs a specialized tip to locally induce or sense phase changes between metallic and insulating states, enabling nanoscale mapping of emergent conduction boundaries and phase coexistence.

“Scanning Ballistic Conduction Microscopy (SBCM),” which uses a specialized tip to locally inject carriers at high energies and measure ballistic transport with minimal scattering, enabling nanoscale mapping of conduction pathways in materials.

“Scanning Piezomagnetic Force Microscopy (SPMFM),” which uses a specialized tip to apply local mechanical stress while measuring the resulting magnetization changes, enabling nanoscale mapping of piezomagnetic domain structures.

“Scanning Proton Conduction Microscopy (SPCM),” which employs a tip capable of locally injecting and detecting proton flow, enabling nanoscale mapping of protonic conductivity and related electrochemical phenomena in materials.

“Scanning Near-Field Radiative Heat Transfer Microscopy (SNRHTM),” which uses a specialized tip to locally measure sub-wavelength thermal radiation exchange with the sample, enabling nanoscale mapping of near-field photon-based heat transfer.

“Scanning Spin-Phonon Coupling Microscopy (SSPCM),” which uses a specialized tip to locally excite and detect spin–lattice interactions, enabling nanoscale mapping of how spins and vibrations couple in quantum materials.

“Scanning Spin-Superfluid Force Microscopy (SSSFM),” which employs a specialized tip to locally excite and detect spin supercurrents, enabling nanoscale mapping of spin-superfluid domains and phase coherence in magnetic or correlated materials.

“Scanning Moiré Lattice Force Microscopy (SMLFM),” which employs a tip sensitive to interlayer coupling and detects local moiré superlattice potentials in twisted 2D heterostructures, enabling nanoscale mapping of twist-angle–dependent electronic properties.

“Scanning Attosecond Tunneling Force Microscopy (SATFM),” which employs sub-femtosecond laser pulses at the tip to drive and detect strong-field electron tunneling forces locally, enabling attosecond-scale temporal resolution of carrier dynamics at the nanoscale.

“Scanning Spin-Glass Domain Microscopy (SSGDM),” which employs a specialized tip to detect slow, frustrated spin dynamics in spin-glass materials, enabling nanoscale mapping of glassy magnetic domains and relaxation processes.

“Scanning Shift-Current Microscopy (SShCM),” which uses a specialized tip with localized optical excitation to measure photogenerated shift currents in non-centrosymmetric materials, enabling nanoscale mapping of bulk photovoltaic effects and broken-inversion symmetries.

“Scanning Non-Abelian Gauge Force Microscopy (SNAGFM),” which employs a specialized tip to locally create or sense non-Abelian gauge fields, enabling nanoscale mapping of exotic topological phenomena and correlated excitations.

“Scanning Quantum Metric Force Microscopy (SQMFM),” which uses a specialized tip to locally measure the quantum metric of electronic states, enabling nanoscale mapping of geometry-driven phenomena in advanced materials.

“Scanning Radiative Recombination Microscopy (SRRM),” which uses a specialized tip to locally excite electron–hole pairs and detect their emitted photons, enabling nanoscale mapping of recombination pathways and defect-induced emission variations in semiconductors.

“Scanning Negative Capacitance Force Microscopy (SNCFM),” which employs a specialized tip to locally probe the transient negative capacitance effect in ferroelectric materials, enabling nanoscale mapping of energy-lowering polarization dynamics.

“Scanning Spin-Nematic Force Microscopy (SSNFM),” which employs a specialized tip to locally probe quadrupolar spin ordering in spin-nematic phases, enabling nanoscale mapping of anisotropic spin correlations.

How could we accelerate density function.answer: One effective approach is to employ GPU-accelerated parallelization, leveraging specialized libraries that distribute and optimize DFT computations across many processing cores.
Utilize linear-scaling or O(N) implementations that partition large systems into localized regions, significantly reducing computational overhead without sacrificing accuracy.
Employ advanced machine-learning-based exchange–correlation functionals that approximate complex integrals, thereby reducing the number of costly computations and accelerating convergence.
Adopt resolution-of-the-identity (RI) or density-fitting methods to reduce the computational overhead of evaluating two-electron integrals, thereby speeding up DFT calculations.

Implement robust preconditioning schemes for the Kohn–Sham equations to significantly improve convergence and reduce the number of self-consistent field iterations, thereby accelerating the overall DFT calculation.
Employ efficient multigrid solvers for the Poisson equation to reduce the computational bottleneck of evaluating electrostatic potentials, thus speeding up DFT calculations for large systems.
Leverage polynomial expansions of the Kohn–Sham Hamiltonian to approximate the Green’s function, thereby bypassing costly diagonalizations and significantly speeding up the DFT procedure.

Adopt advanced iterative diagonalization techniques (e.g., Davidson or Lanczos methods) with efficient parallelization to rapidly converge Kohn–Sham eigenstates, significantly reducing the computational cost of DFT calculations.

Implement a fast multipole method for efficiently computing long-range Coulomb interactions in real space, thereby reducing the computational scaling and significantly speeding up large-scale DFT calculations.
Utilize wavelet-based real-space formalisms that adaptively refine the grid around regions of interest, significantly reducing the computational load and accelerating DFT for complex systems.
Employ density matrix embedding theory (DMET) to partition the system into correlated subspaces, substantially lowering computational overhead while preserving the accuracy of DFT calculations.
Implement a caching or reuse strategy for frequently computed integrals, significantly reducing redundant calculations and thereby speeding up DFT simulations.

Utilize the adaptively compressed exchange (ACE) operator in hybrid DFT calculations to reduce the computational cost of evaluating Fock exchange, significantly speeding up calculations without sacrificing accuracy.
Adopt optimized localized basis sets to minimize the total number of functions, thereby reducing integral evaluations and computational overhead, which accelerates DFT calculations.
Adopt hierarchical matrix compression to reduce the rank of large operators, significantly lowering memory requirements and computational costs in DFT simulations.

Employ a warm-start scheme that reuses wavefunctions or potentials from simpler initial calculations (e.g., smaller basis sets) to generate better starting guesses for the Kohn–Sham SCF cycle, thereby reducing the number of iterations and accelerating DFT computations.
Adopt domain decomposition strategies that split the system into smaller subdomains and solve each in parallel, thus reducing the scaling bottleneck and speeding up DFT calculations.
Use pseudopotentials or effective core potentials to avoid explicitly treating core electrons, thereby reducing the computational burden and accelerating DFT calculations.

Adopt orbital-free DFT approximations that bypass expensive Kohn–Sham orbital evaluations, substantially reducing the computational effort while still capturing essential electron-density effects for large-scale systems.

Use integral screening techniques with optimally chosen thresholds to discard negligible contributions, thereby reducing the number of evaluated integrals and significantly speeding up DFT computations.

Use sophisticated fractional-occupancy smearing schemes (e.g., cold smearing) to smooth out electronic states near the Fermi level, helping the self-consistent field procedure converge faster and thus accelerating DFT calculations, particularly for metallic systems.

Use advanced k-point sampling or interpolation techniques (e.g., symmetry reduction or adaptive k-meshes) to minimize the number of k-points needed for accurate results, thereby reducing computation time and accelerating DFT calculations.

Adopt density functional tight-binding (DFTB) methods, which employ simplified Hamiltonians and parameterized integrals to substantially reduce computational complexity and accelerate large-scale DFT calculations.

Adopt a multi-functional SCF scheme where the calculation begins with a simpler functional (e.g., LDA) to rapidly approximate the density, then switches to the target functional for final refinement, reducing the total number of expensive iterations and accelerating DFT overall.

Leverage machine-learning models to generate accurate initial electron densities or wavefunctions directly from structural data, reducing the number of self-consistent field iterations and thereby speeding up DFT calculations.

Adopt a hybrid QM/MM approach so that only the region of interest is treated at the DFT level while the surrounding environment is handled with classical force fields, substantially reducing the computational load.
Adopt advanced density mixing algorithms (e.g., Pulay or Broyden schemes) to rapidly converge the self-consistent field iterations and thereby accelerate DFT simulations.

Employ real-time time-propagation methods to relax the electron density into the ground state, eliminating certain iterative steps found in traditional SCF procedures and thereby accelerating DFT calculations for large systems.

Adopt direct orbital minimization methods (e.g., conjugate gradient) that bypass expensive Hamiltonian diagonalizations, enabling faster self-consistent convergence and thus accelerating DFT calculations.

Employ wavefunction extrapolation schemes during geometry optimizations or molecular dynamics, reusing previously converged solutions to generate improved initial guesses, thereby reducing the required SCF iterations and accelerating DFT calculations.

Implement single-precision or mixed-precision algorithms, carefully controlling numerical accuracy, to reduce memory usage and speed up floating-point operations, thereby accelerating DFT calculations.

Adopt subsystem DFT with frozen density embedding to treat localized fragments independently, substantially lowering computational expenses without sacrificing overall accuracy for large-scale systems.

Deploy specialized hardware accelerators beyond traditional GPUs, such as FPGAs or tensor processing units, to speed up core linear algebra operations in DFT codes, substantially enhancing parallel performance and reducing run times.

Adopt an asynchronous self-consistent field approach that updates localized portions of the density in parallel and only synchronizes intermittently, thereby reducing communication overhead and accelerating large-scale DFT simulations.
Adopt pseudo-spectral methods that evaluate exchange–correlation integrals at discrete collocation points, reducing the number of integrals and thus significantly speeding up DFT computations.

Leverage emerging quantum computing subroutines for particularly expensive tasks (e.g., certain integral evaluations), thereby offloading and reducing key computational bottlenecks to accelerate overall DFT simulations.

Implement a machine-learning surrogate model for the two-electron integrals, allowing the code to bypass repeated integral evaluations and thus substantially reduce the overall computational effort in DFT calculations.

Implement dynamic load balancing strategies that continuously monitor and redistribute tasks among compute nodes in real time, ensuring high resource utilization and thereby accelerating large-scale DFT calculations.
Adopt optimized all-to-all communication patterns in plane-wave DFT codes to minimize expensive data transposes, significantly reducing parallel overhead and accelerating large-scale calculations.

Adopt high-order finite-difference discretizations in real space, which enable more accurate derivative approximations with fewer grid points, thereby reducing memory requirements and accelerating DFT calculations.

Implement efficient parallel I/O libraries (e.g., using HDF5 or parallel NetCDF) that distribute large wavefunction or density data across multiple nodes, significantly reducing disk bottlenecks and thereby speeding up large-scale DFT calculations.

Use a stochastic DFT approach, where selected integrals or matrix elements are randomly sampled rather than fully computed, thereby reducing the scaling bottleneck and accelerating calculations for large systems.

Use a reinforcement learning scheme to dynamically tune SCF parameters (e.g., mixing rates, convergence thresholds) during runtime, thereby reducing the number of iterations required and accelerating overall DFT calculations.

Adopt a discontinuous Galerkin approach for solving the Kohn–Sham equations, which supports local polynomial expansions and efficient parallelization, thereby reducing computational complexity and accelerating DFT calculations.

Adopt an adaptively pruned plane-wave basis, discarding components with negligible electron-density contributions and thus reducing the basis size—this lowers computational costs while maintaining accuracy, accelerating DFT evaluations.
Adopt range-separated hybrid functionals that confine Fock exchange to short-range interactions, thereby reducing overall scaling and significantly accelerating large-scale DFT calculations.
Adopt continuum solvation models (e.g., PCM or COSMO) to replace explicit solvent molecules with an implicit dielectric environment, thereby reducing system size and accelerating DFT simulations.

Leverage modern CPU vectorization (e.g., AVX-512) and tuned BLAS/LAPACK libraries to accelerate core linear algebra routines in DFT codes, thus reducing computational time by maximizing throughput on existing processors.

Implement a nonuniform fast Fourier transform (NFFT) scheme in plane-wave DFT codes to efficiently handle locally dense regions or vacuum, thus reducing grid sizes and data-movement overhead, and ultimately accelerating the overall computation.

Adopt selective eigenspace algorithms that compute only the occupied orbitals required for ground-state properties, omitting higher-energy states to reduce diagonalization overhead and thereby accelerate DFT calculations.

Use a data-driven surrogate for the potential energy surface (e.g., a machine-learned force field) so that fewer expensive self-consistent DFT evaluations are required during geometry optimizations or molecular dynamics, thus substantially accelerating the overall calculation.

Adopt a domain-based local pair natural orbital (DLPNO) approach that confines the wavefunction to localized atomic pairs, thereby substantially reducing the number of integrals and accelerating DFT calculations while preserving accuracy.

Implement wavefunction or density-matrix compression techniques to reduce both storage and I/O overhead, thereby accelerating large-scale DFT simulations by minimizing data transfer and memory requirements.

Adopt systematic basis-set extrapolation, performing lower-cost calculations in smaller basis sets and then extrapolating to the complete-basis-set limit, thereby avoiding repeated large-scale runs and significantly accelerating the overall DFT workflow.

Adopt density matrix purification methods that iteratively refine the approximate density matrix using polynomial expansions, thereby avoiding explicit diagonalization and significantly accelerating DFT calculations.

Adopt a dynamic plane-wave cutoff strategy, starting DFT calculations with a lower cutoff to cheaply approximate the electron density and gradually raising it to the final required value only as convergence progresses, thereby reducing the total computational effort without compromising the accuracy of the final result.

Adopt a unified HPC solver framework (e.g., ELSI) that integrates multiple diagonalization or density-matrix solvers and automatically selects the optimal backend for a given system, thereby maximizing performance and accelerating DFT calculations.

Use a machine-learning approach to automatically predict and adjust the optimal plane-wave cutoff or basis set size from the system’s structure, avoiding overly conservative parameters and thereby reducing the computational load while maintaining accuracy.

Adopt an automatic differentiation framework to directly compute derivative information in DFT codes, thereby accelerating geometry optimizations and reducing computational costs associated with evaluating gradients or forces.

Adopt an augmented plane-wave plus local orbitals (APW+lo) framework that supplements plane waves with minimal local basis functions for core states, reducing the required cutoff while preserving accuracy and thereby accelerating DFT calculations.

Employ maximally localized Wannier functions to convert extended wavefunctions into localized orbitals, thereby confining interactions to smaller regions and significantly reducing the computational cost of evaluating integrals in DFT computations.

Implement adaptive quadrature schemes for the exchange–correlation integrals that dynamically refine high-variation regions of the electron density, reducing the total number of integration points required while maintaining accuracy and thus accelerating DFT calculations.

Implement specialized checkpointing of partial SCF solutions so that, when a system’s geometry or spin state changes, the calculation restarts from an intermediate converged density rather than beginning anew, thereby saving iterations and accelerating large-scale DFT computations.

Adopt tensor hypercontraction to rewrite two-electron integrals in a low-rank factorized form, thereby drastically reducing both computational cost and memory footprint and accelerating DFT calculations.
Fully exploit point-group or space-group symmetry to block diagonalize the Kohn–Sham Hamiltonian, reducing the effective system dimension and thereby accelerating DFT calculations.

Use batched linear algebra routines for frequently occurring small matrix operations (e.g., in sub-block computations), ensuring that data remains in cache and thus reducing overhead, which significantly speeds up DFT calculations.

Implement a dedicated band-parallelization strategy that assigns distinct subsets of Kohn–Sham eigenstates to different processors, enabling concurrent diagonalization steps and significantly accelerating large-scale DFT calculations.

Implement a neural PDE solver that directly approximates solutions to the Kohn–Sham equations, thereby bypassing many iterative steps in the self-consistent field procedure and significantly accelerating DFT calculations.

Adopt a many-body expansion technique that systematically partitions the system into smaller fragments whose energies are independently computed and combined, thereby reducing computational complexity and enabling highly parallel DFT calculations.

Adopt a multiple-scattering approach (e.g., KKR method) that solves the electronic structure through scattering matrices instead of large basis expansions, often requiring fewer basis functions and reducing computational costs for certain material classes.

Implement a compressive sensing approach to reconstruct the electron density from a sparse set of sampling points, thereby reducing the computational effort required for exchange–correlation evaluations and speeding up DFT calculations.

Implement a just-in-time code generation framework that automatically tailors integral and matrix operations to the underlying hardware and system parameters, thereby minimizing kernel overhead and substantially accelerating DFT computations.

Adopt an inexact Newton–Krylov scheme for the SCF iteration, where the linearized equations are solved only approximately at each step, thereby reducing diagonalization costs while maintaining robust convergence and accelerating DFT calculations.

Adopt the Harris functional (or Harris–Foulkes) approach as a single-step approximation to the Kohn–Sham cycle. This method bypasses many iterative updates by constructing a density and potential from a fixed guess, thereby significantly lowering the computational overhead in DFT calculations.

Implement a strictly sparse real‐space Hamiltonian representation alongside specialized sparse linear algebra routines that exploit locality in the electron density, thereby reducing memory usage and significantly accelerating large‐scale DFT computations.

Implement a fused-kernel strategy that combines multiple steps (e.g., density evaluation and exchange–correlation integration) into a single pass, improving data locality and reducing memory transfers, thereby accelerating DFT calculations.

Adopt B-spline expansions in real space, which can flexibly adapt to local features with fewer basis functions than conventional plane-wave methods, thereby reducing the computational overhead and accelerating large-scale DFT calculations.

Implement multiple time-step integrators (e.g., rRESPA) during ab initio molecular dynamics so that the costly DFT force evaluations occur less frequently, while cheaper interactions are updated more often, thereby reducing the overall computational overhead.

Adopt partial Cholesky decomposition of two-electron integrals, factorizing the Coulomb operator into low-rank expansions that significantly reduce the overall computational scaling, thereby accelerating DFT calculations.
Adopt Car–Parrinello molecular dynamics, where electronic and ionic degrees of freedom are evolved together, reducing repeated SCF cycles at each step and thereby accelerating DFT-based simulations.

Adopt a multi-stage integration grid for the exchange–correlation terms, starting with a coarse grid during initial SCF cycles to cheaply approximate the density, then switching to a finer grid in the final steps. This reduces the cost of repeated integral evaluations without compromising the final accuracy.

Adopt a reduced-order modeling approach that constructs a compact representation of the Kohn–Sham Hamiltonian (e.g., via projection onto a low-dimensional subspace), thereby greatly reducing the size of the eigenvalue problem and accelerating DFT computations.

Adopt the pole expansion and selected inversion (PEXSI) method to bypass large-scale diagonalizations. By approximating the Fermi–Dirac function with a set of complex poles and then performing sparse “selected inversion” of the Hamiltonian, PEXSI reduces computational scaling and accelerates DFT calculations for large systems.

Adopt a combined Gaussian and plane-wave (GPW) approach, which employs localized Gaussians for the electronic orbitals while using plane waves to handle electrostatics, thereby reducing the basis-set size and overall computational overhead, and thus accelerating DFT calculations.

Implement a DMRG-based approach for the Kohn–Sham orbitals, compressing the system’s wavefunction or density matrix through low-entanglement representations that greatly reduce computational costs while maintaining accuracy, thereby accelerating DFT calculations for large or strongly correlated systems.

Leverage an on-the-fly, self-adaptive basis set re-optimization strategy that refines the localized basis functions as the system’s geometry evolves, thereby minimizing both basis size and integral evaluations and substantially accelerating DFT calculations.

Use an electronegativity equalization method (EEM) to build an approximate starting electron density before running Kohn–Sham iterations, thereby reducing SCF cycles and accelerating the overall DFT calculation.

Implement partial reorthogonalization of the wavefunctions so that only the components most prone to drift are re-orthonormalized at each iteration, reducing the overhead of full orthogonalization and thereby accelerating large-scale DFT calculations.

Use electron difference potentials to compute only the change in the electron density from a reference state (e.g., a known structure) when exploring nearby geometries, thereby avoiding full recalculation of all terms and significantly accelerating DFT-based scans or optimizations.

Adopt a spectral element method for solving the Kohn–Sham equations, wherein high-order polynomial expansions over local subdomains yield rapid convergence and excellent parallel efficiency, thereby significantly reducing the computational cost of DFT calculations.

Use a Γ-point–only scheme with real wavefunctions (when symmetry allows), which halves memory requirements by avoiding complex orbitals and significantly reduces the computational overhead, thereby accelerating DFT calculations.

Adopt the Absolutely Localized Molecular Orbitals (ALMO) approach to confine orbitals strictly to predefined fragments, which reduces the coupling between fragments and thereby lowers the overall computational complexity, significantly accelerating large-scale DFT calculations.

Implement a level‐shifting technique that artificially raises the energy of unoccupied states, stabilizing the SCF cycle (especially for metals or small‐gap systems) and thus shortening convergence times and accelerating the overall DFT calculation.

Adopt a real‐space framework that expands wavefunctions in a radial basis function (RBF) set, adaptively refining resolution near atomic cores; this often requires fewer basis functions than uniform plane‐wave methods, reducing both computational costs and memory demands to accelerate DFT calculations.

Provide an explanation for the role of \.answer: So-called “junk DNA,” once thought to have no purpose, often plays important regulatory roles, helping control gene expression, maintain chromosome structure, and influence evolutionary processes.
Though once considered nonfunctional, these noncoding regions help regulate gene activity, maintain structural integrity of chromosomes, and provide a source of new genetic variation for evolution.

What new synthetic DNA base pairs can le.answer: There are currently no scientifically validated synthetic DNA base pairs capable of enhancing human intelligence.

No synthetic DNA base pair currently exists that is scientifically demonstrated to enhance human intelligence, as research on artificially expanded genetic alphabets has yet to establish any link to improved cognition.

Hypothesize how to make an alive organis.answer: Creating a true human-tree hybrid is currently beyond scientific feasibility due to fundamental differences between plant and animal biology. In a purely hypothetical scenario, one might attempt to blend plant genes (responsible for traits like photosynthesis or cell wall structure) with human cells via advanced synthetic biology tools like CRISPR, followed by in-lab development of a chimeric embryo under highly controlled conditions. However, such a method remains ethically and legally untenable, and existing technology cannot support it beyond speculative theory.

One speculative concept would be to genetically engineer human stem cells to express plant-like traits—such as specialized chloroplast analogs or protective cell-wall-like membranes—by using synthetic biology tools to integrate carefully selected plant genes. In theory, these lab-grown chimeric cells would be coaxed to form composite tissues blending human and plant structures. However, this remains profoundly unfeasible with current technology and raises significant ethical and biological challenges.

What is the origin of homochirality in b.answer: It is widely thought that small initial chiral imbalances—likely introduced by external factors such as polarized light or asymmetric catalysts—were amplified through autocatalytic processes to produce the uniform chirality observed in today’s biomolecules.
An early random bias toward one mirror form became locked in by self-reinforcing reactions and evolutionary selection, establishing the single-handedness of biological molecules.

A plausible view is that slight stochastic asymmetries in early molecular systems were reinforced by replicative and metabolic steps, eventually yielding the exclusive chirality observed in living organisms.

Describe a synthetic genetic circuit tha.answer: A synthetic genetic circuit could use magnetosensitive cryptochrome proteins to detect quantum spin interactions. By linking cryptochrome to a reporter gene in bacteria, changes in the radical pair mechanism under subtle magnetic fields would alter reporter output. This shift in gene expression (e.g., fluorescence) would provide a built-in readout of quantum-level magnetic fluctuations.

A possible design would incorporate the Fenna–Matthews–Olson (FMO) protein from green sulfur bacteria, which exhibits quantum coherence during energy transfer. Engineered bacteria could express FMO along with a reporter gene whose activation depends on FMO’s exciton transfer efficiency. Small shifts in coherence, influenced by quantum effects, would trigger the reporter, enabling detection and measurement of quantum-level fluctuations.

A possible design is to engineer cells expressing an enzyme known to exhibit hydrogen tunneling (e.g., a variant of soybean lipoxygenase) whose catalytic rate is especially sensitive to quantum effects. The circuit links the enzyme’s product level to a downstream transcription factor and reporter gene. Subtle shifts in tunneling rates—caused by changes in quantum phenomena—alter product concentration, which modulates the reporter output and provides a direct biosensor for quantum-level fluctuations.

A feasible design uses engineered cells expressing Photosystem II (PSII) reaction centers, which exhibit quantum coherence during initial charge separation. The cells co-express a redox-sensitive transcription factor controlling a fluorescent reporter gene. Small fluctuations in PSII’s coherence affect electron flow, shifting the redox balance and altering transcription factor activity, thus providing a measurable output linked to quantum-level processes.

A synthetic circuit could incorporate the light-harvesting complex 2 (LHC2) from purple photosynthetic bacteria, which exhibits quantum coherence during exciton transport. By engineering cells to express LHC2 linked to a redox-sensitive transcription factor and reporter, small variations in coherence (e.g., caused by subtle external fields) would alter the redox state and trigger a measurable shift in reporter output—providing a biological quantum sensor.

One viable design utilizes quantum-coherent electron transfer within Photosystem I (PSI) from cyanobacteria, engineered into cells along with a redox-sensitive transcription factor controlling a reporter gene. Tiny fluctuations in quantum coherence within PSI alter downstream redox states, modulating the transcription factor and leading to changes in reporter expression—thus serving as a biological quantum sensor.

Suggest a way to create artificial organ.answer: One approach is to engineer lipid-coated nanoreactors that embed critical enzymes, mimicking essential metabolic functions. By localizing and regulating specific biochemical reactions within these synthetic compartments, cells can boost efficiency and enhance overall metabolism.

Construct polymer-based vesicles loaded with specialized enzymes that selectively catalyze vital reactions. These synthetic compartments can interface with the cell’s metabolic pathways, offering controlled exchange of substrates and products, thus increasing overall efficiency.

Encapsulate key enzymes within re-engineered protein-based microcompartments—such as viral capsids or bacterial compartments—designed to self-assemble inside cells, thereby creating highly efficient synthetic organelles to boost metabolic performance.

Develop layer-by-layer nanoshells around key enzymes using electrostatic self-assembly. These shells form discrete, permeable compartments that can be introduced into cells, enabling localized reactions and improving metabolic throughput.

Develop programmable DNA nanocompartments that house crucial enzymes and cofactors, creating discrete, permeable spaces for optimized substrate channeling and improved metabolic throughput within cells.

Design a self-assembling peptide-based scaffold that clusters critical enzymes and cofactors into stable microdomains within the cell. By physically concentrating and protecting these enzymes while facilitating substrate flow, these synthetic organelles can accelerate metabolic reactions and enhance cellular energy efficiency.

Propose a synthetic chromosome design th.answer: A concise design could include Deinococcus radiodurans–derived DNA repair genes (e.g., recA, pprA) and enhanced antioxidant enzyme genes (e.g., superoxide dismutase) placed under strong, radiation-inducible promoters on a synthetic mini-chromosome. This arrangement would provide robust DNA repair and oxidative stress protection, improving cellular viability under high-radiation conditions.

Construct a synthetic mini-chromosome encoding the tardigrade-derived Dsup (Damage Suppressor) gene under a radiation-inducible promoter, ensuring that cells rapidly synthesize protective Dsup proteins upon exposure and thereby reduce DNA breakage and enhance survival under high-radiation conditions.

Design a synthetic mini-chromosome incorporating key Thermococcus gammatolerans repair factors (e.g., advanced recombinase homologs) under a radiation-responsive promoter, coupled with specialized archaea-derived antioxidant enzymes to rapidly neutralize reactive species. This coordinated system would bolster DNA repair and oxidative stress resistance, thereby enhancing the cell’s ability to withstand high-radiation environments.

Design a synthetic chromosome that encodes a fungal melanin biosynthesis pathway (e.g., from Cryptococcus neoformans) under a radiation-inducible promoter, allowing cells to produce protective melanin upon exposure. Pair this with targeted DNA repair factors on the same chromosome, ensuring complementary defense against radiation-induced damage.

Design a synthetic chromosome that encodes Bacillus subtilis spore coat proteins for protective encapsulation, coupled with a suite of strongly regulated DNA repair enzymes under a radiation-inducible promoter. This enables a “spore-like” shield around cellular DNA and rapid recovery from radiation-induced damage.

Design a synthetic chromosome encoding key Rubrobacter radiotolerans DNA repair enzymes (e.g., specialized polymerases) and robust free radical scavengers (e.g., catalase-peroxidase) under a high-sensitivity, radiation-inducible promoter, enabling rapid repair of DNA double-strand breaks and prompt neutralization of reactive species to enhance cellular survival under radiation stress.

Suggest a biological process or system t.answer: Magnetoreception in birds, specifically the cryptochrome protein, which appears to use quantum effects for navigation.
The Fenna–Matthews–Olson (FMO) complex in green sulfur bacteria, which exploits quantum coherence in photosynthesis and could be developed for quantum sensing.
Soybean lipoxygenase, known to employ hydrogen tunneling, could be adapted for quantum sensing due to its inherent sensitivity to quantum effects.
Nitrogenase in nitrogen-fixing bacteria, which relies on precise quantum tunneling for electron and proton transfer, could be adapted for quantum sensing applications.
The photosynthetic reaction center in purple bacteria, such as Rhodobacter sphaeroides, demonstrates quantum coherence in electron transfer and could be adapted for quantum sensing.
Light-harvesting complex II (LHCII) in higher plants, which shows quantum coherence in energy transfer, could be adapted for quantum sensing applications.
Bacteriorhodopsin in halophilic archaea, which relies on precise photo-induced retinal isomerization, could potentially be adapted for quantum sensing.
Cytochrome c, which relies on quantum tunneling in electron transfer, could be adapted for quantum sensing applications.
Photosystem I in cyanobacteria, which exhibits ultrafast electron transfer under quantum coherence, could be adapted for quantum sensing.

Propose a biological experiment to test.answer: Set up a sealed chamber with a non-aqueous solvent (e.g., formamide or liquid ammonia), add prebiotic building blocks (amino acids, nucleobases, and simple lipids), and expose the mixture to energy sources (UV light or electrical sparks) under simulated early-planet conditions; then monitor for increasing molecular complexity and self-assembly into vesicle-like structures or catalytic networks as evidence for potential proto-life.

Create a microfluidic system where a non-aqueous solvent (like formamide) flows past various prebiotic compounds alongside catalysts. Vary temperature and other conditions in different channels, then continuously monitor reaction products using spectroscopy or mass spectrometry. Look for increasing molecular complexity or self-assembling structures that might signal life-like processes emerging in a non-aqueous environment.

Construct a low-temperature reactor simulating Titan-like conditions (methane or ethane as the solvent) and introduce simple organic precursors known to exist on Titan’s surface or in its atmosphere. Subject the mixture to controlled temperature and pressure cycles, as well as irradiation mimicking cosmic rays or solar UV. Regularly sample the reactor’s contents via mass spectrometry or similar methods to monitor for the formation of complex organics or metabolic-like activity. Evidence of self-assembly or catalysis in these cryogenic solvents would support the hypothesis that life could originate in non-aqueous environments.

Establish a closed reactor using an ionic liquid (instead of water) and introduce simple organic building blocks (amino acids, nucleobases, and lipid-like molecules). Cycle temperature and pressure within the reactor to promote polymerization and self-assembly. Regularly sample and analyze the reactor contents using spectroscopy and microscopic imaging to detect any emergent structures or catalytic processes that resemble early life-like behavior.

Place simple organic precursors and mineral catalysts in a high-pressure vessel containing supercritical carbon dioxide as the sole solvent. Gradually vary the temperature and pressure while applying an external energy source (e.g., UV or electrical discharge), then periodically sample to detect any newly formed complex polymers or self-assembling structures indicative of life-like processes in a non-aqueous environment.

Use a deep eutectic solvent (e.g., choline chloride–urea) in a temperature-controlled reactor with simple organic monomers and mineral catalysts. Vary temperature and provide energy sources, such as UV or thermal cycling, then regularly analyze samples via spectroscopy and microscopy to detect any complex polymerization, self-assembly, or catalytic behavior suggesting a pathway toward life in a non-aqueous medium.

Place a molten alkali salt mixture in a sealed high-temperature reactor as the solvent. Add simple organics (e.g., amino nitriles) and mineral catalysts (e.g., iron sulfides), then apply temperature cycling to drive chemical reactions. Periodically sample the reactor’s contents using spectroscopy and other analytical methods to identify any formation of complex organics or self-assembling structures indicative of life-like processes in a non-aqueous environment.

Set up a sealed chamber with a silicone-based fluid as the solvent and introduce simple organic precursors (e.g., amino acids, nucleotides, lipid-like molecules) along with mineral catalysts. Provide an energy source (e.g., UV irradiation) and cycle temperature to promote chemical reactions. Periodically sample the chamber’s contents using analytical methods (e.g., spectroscopy, microscopy) to detect any emergence of polymers or self-assembling structures that could indicate early life-like processes in a non-aqueous environment.
